coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanopomegranate for selective detection of quinoline in coking wastewater

coredata.dc:description: Quinoline, as a refractory and toxic organic pollutant in coking wastewater, causes great harm to the environment and human health even in trace amount. To realize the selective and sensitive detection of quinoline in coking wastewater, a novel molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanopomegranate with carbon dots (CDs) as seeds and fluorescence source (CD-MIP) was prepared, using quinoline as the template, and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (KH792) as the monomer. The preparation and detection conditions of CD-MIP were systematically optimized. The structure and detection performance of CD-MIP were investigated in detail. The resulting CD-MIP exhibits excellent photoluminescence performance, high detection sensitivity, good selectivity and reproducibility towards quinoline. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CD-MIP shows a satisfying linearity with quinoline concentration in the range of 20–200 mg/L with a detection limit of 6.7 mg/L. Owing to the existence of imprinted cavities that highly match with quinoline, a high imprinting factor (3.46) for CD-MIP was obtained. In addition, CD-MIP represents a greater affinity towards quinoline than towards other analogues, as well as an outstanding anti-interference capability. For trace analysis in real coking wastewater, CD-MIP also gives satisfactory results. Therefore, CD-MIP shows promising application in the selective detection of trace quinoline in wastewater.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-based molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanopomegranate for selective detection of quinoline in coking wastewater

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots were embedded as seeds in molecularly imprinted polymer matrix to construct a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent nanopomegranate by combining molecular imprinting technology with carbon dot-based fluorescence detection technology for detecting trace concentration of quinoline in coking wastewater.

Quinoline, as a refractory and toxic organic pollutant in coking wastewater, causes great harm to the environment and human health even in trace amount. To realize the selective and sensitive detection of quinoline in coking wastewater, a novel molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanopomegranate with carbon dots (CDs) as seeds and fluorescence source (CD-MIP) was prepared, using quinoline as the template, and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (KH792) as the monomer. The preparation and detection conditions of CD-MIP were systematically optimized. The structure and detection performance of CD-MIP were investigated in detail. The resulting CD-MIP exhibits excellent photoluminescence performance, high detection sensitivity, good selectivity and reproducibility towards quinoline. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CD-MIP shows a satisfying linearity with quinoline concentration in the range of 20–200 mg/L with a detection limit of 6.7 mg/L. Owing to the existence of imprinted cavities that highly match with quinoline, a high imprinting factor (3.46) for CD-MIP was obtained. In addition, CD-MIP represents a greater affinity towards quinoline than towards other analogues, as well as an outstanding anti-interference capability. For trace analysis in real coking wastewater, CD-MIP also gives satisfactory results. Therefore, CD-MIP shows promising application in the selective detection of trace quinoline in wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Coking wastewater pollution has always been a challenging topic of environmental protection owing to its complex, toxic, and refractory composition, especially the existence of nitrogen heterocyclic compounds (NHCs) [1,2]. Quinoline, as one of the typical toxic and refractory organic pollutants in NHCs, accounts for a large proportion of the total content of NHCs [3,4]. As it is a strong carcinogen, short exposure to quinoline vapors can cause corrosion of nose, eyes, and respiratory tract; prolonged exposure has been closely correlated with liver injury [5–7]. In order to avoid these hazards, the emission standard of NHCs pollutants for coking chemical industry is set as no higher than 10 mg/L.

So far, many conventional analytical methods such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry (MS), and electrochemical method have been applied to the sensitive detection of quinoline with satisfying results [8]. However, in HPLC and MS, the use of mobile phase and large amount of organic solvent is inevitable, along with necessary extraction, filtration, and derivation treatment. These factors result in high operating requirements, lengthy analysis time, sophisticated instrumentation, complex of sample pre-treatment, and expensive cost. For electrochemical method, its poor stability and reproducibility in trace analysis put much limit on its application. Thus, it is imperative to develop a fast, selective, and sensitive analytical method for detecting quinoline in complex coking wastewater matrix.

As a new analysis and detection technology, molecular imprinting technology has found wider and wider applications. By this technology, molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) can be prepared, possessing highly matchable imprinted cavities with template molecule in shape, size, and functional groups [9–11]. Because of the three characteristics of structure predetermination, wide applicability, and specific recognition [12], MIP has been widely used to specifically and selectively recognize and enrich target molecule from extremely low concentration complex matrix [13–15].

Fluorescence detection technology can be used for qualitative or quantitative analysis of target analyte based on the interaction between detector and target analyte [11,16,17]. It has drawn an increasing attention by virtue of its unique advantages including high sensitivity, simple instrumentation, and easy detection [18–20]. For fluorescence detection, suitable fluorescence materials are vital [21]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of carbon-based fluorescence nanoparticles, have been increasingly used in fluorescence detection owing to their excellent optical and chemical properties, simple synthesis, excellent water solubility, environmental friendliness, and tunable photoluminescence property [22–26].

In this work, a novel molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanopomegranate (CD-MIP), which integrates the specific recognition of MIP towards target molecule and the sensitive fluorescence characteristics of CDs, was presented for the selective detection of quinoline in coking wastewater [27,28]. In this way, not only high sensitivity can be maintained, but also high selectivity and anti-interference can be guaranteed [29,30]. In the resultant nanopomegranate structure, CDs were used as the seeds for fluorescence signal output, around which surface molecularly imprinted polymeric shell was created with quinoline and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (KH792) as the template molecule and functional monomer, respectively. The preparation method of CDs, the key preparation conditions of CD-MIP, and the detection conditions of CD-MIP towards quinoline were systematically discussed. The fluorescence stability, detection range, detection mechanism, and selectivity of CD-MIP towards quinoline in water were studied in detail.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA), γ-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (KH550), N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methylbimethoxysilane (KH602), KH792, ethanol (99.7 %), ammonia solution (25 %), pyridine (95 %), and carbazole (98 %) were supplied by Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., ltd. (China). Quinoline (99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd. (China). Methanol (99.5 %) and acetic acid (99.5 %) were supplied by Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (China). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99 %), phenol (95 %), and naphthalene (98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. (China). All the reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

CDs were synthesized by the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method described in our previous work [31]. The experimental procedures are provided in the Supplementary Material. To screen the optimal preparation method of CDs, three kinds of raw material (KH550, KH602, and KH792) and two preparation methods (hydrothermal method and microwave-assisted hydrothermal method) were used to optimize the preparation method of CDs with the highest quenching efficiency towards quinoline [32,33].

As shown in Fig. 1 , CD-MIP was fabricated according to a one-pot strategy. First, 100 μL of CD solution, 118 μL of quinoline, 324 μL of KH792, and 30 mL of ethanol were added to a three-neck flask. The mixture solution was sonicated for 5 min and purged with Ar for 5 min to eliminate O2, and then stirred at 25 ˚C for 30 min. Afterwards, 896 μL of TEOS and 1 mL of ammonia solution (25 %) were added. The polymerization was performed at 25 ˚C for 24 h. The product was eluted with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, by volume) to completely remove quinoline by breaking the weak hydrogen bond between quinoline and the polymer. Finally, the imprinted fluorescent product with nanopomegranate structure was washed and dried at 50 ˚C, denoted as CD-MIP.

For comparison, non-imprinted counterpart (CD-NIP) and non-fluorescent counterpart molecularly imprinted nanoparticles (MIP without CDs) were fabricated under the same conditions of CD-MIP except that quinoline or CDs were not added, respectively [34,35].

To demonstrate the interaction and affinity between the template quinoline and the functional monomer KH792, the molecular structure optimization and simulation calculation were performed. The structures of quioline, KH792, and quinoline-KH792 complex were optimized, and the binding energy between quinoline and KH792 was calculated. The processes were all conducted by the Materials Studio software, with a B3LYP hybrid functional method in Density Functional Theory (DFT).

The microstructure of the samples was analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2010, Japan). The average particle size of the samples was determined from the TEM images by using Image J software. The particle size distribution curve was obtained by Origin software. The surface functional groups of the samples were characterized with KBr pellet by Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR; BRUKER TENSOR 27, German) over a range of 400–4000 cm−1. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a Differential Thermal Analyzer (DTG-60AH, 100–800 ˚C, 10 ˚C min−1, N2). The crystal structure of the samples was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD; 2θ = 10°-80°, λ = 1.5406 Å, 6° min−1). The elemental composition was analyzed on an Elementar (Vario EL CUBE, He, 950–1200 ˚C, Germany). UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra were measured by a Jinghua 756MC UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were scanned by using a Horiba Fluoromax-4 luminescence spectrometer.

In all the detection experiments, simulated coking wastewater was prepared with deionized water as solvent. The detection experiments were carried out under the conditions of excitation wavelength 365 nm, excitation and emission slit widths 10 nm, and photomultiplier tube voltage 400 V. The working solutions were prepared by dispersing 5, 10, 15, and 20 mg of CD-MIP or CD-NIP into 10 mL of deionized water. The pH value of the CD-MIP working solution was adjusted to 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 with HCl or NaOH solutions. Quinoline standard solution was prepared from a high concentration quinoline aqueous solution. Phenol, pyridine, naphthalene, and carbazole solutions were prepared to determine the selectivity of CD-MIP towards quinoline. In addition, to simulate the complex coking wastewater matrix, four mixed standard solutions of quinoline + phenol, quinoline + pyridine, quinoline + naphthalene, and quinoline + carbazole were prepared. Subsequently, different volumes of standard solution were mixed with the working solutions separately. After a few minutes, the fluorescence spectra of each mixture were recorded. All detection experiments were repeated 3–5 times to give the average and standard deviation. The results were averaged. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is lower than 3.9 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of quinoline in real coking wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The real coking wastewater samples were collected from the secondary sedimentation tank of Minguang Coking Co. ltd. (Quwo County, Linfen City, Shanxi Province, China). The main components of wastewater include phenol, quinoline, and other organic pollutants, ammonia nitrogen and inorganic salts. At 25 ˚C, the wastewater was weakly alkaline (pH = 8). The samples were filtered through 0.22 μm membranes to remove insoluble components. The spiked recovery experiments were carried out by using standard addition method. Typically, quinoline standard solutions with specified concentrations were spiked into the coking wastewater containing CD-MIP. The content of quinoline was determined by fluorescence spectrometry. The relative recovery and RSD were obtained by repeating the experiment 5 times under identical conditions.

The detection performance of fluorescent CD-MIP is mainly affected by the fluorescent CDs, functional monomer, crosslinker, reaction time, and other factors. Among them, the fluorescent CDs mainly determine the sensitivity and detection limit, and the imprinted layer mainly determines the specificity and selectivity. To obtain a CD-MIP with excellent detection performance, it is critical to screen the most suitable preparation conditions.

The sensitivity of CD-MIP mainly depends on the interaction between fluorescent CDs and analyte, which can cause a strong fluorescence response. Selecting appropriate CDs towards analyte is essential for improving the detection sensitivity of CD-MIP. The optimization of the preparation method of CDs is shown inTable 1 . With CA as the carbon source and silane coupling agent as the modifier to prepare CDs, the optimization aims to eliminate extra silanization process and facilitate subsequent imprinting process [36]. In addition, the N and O content of silane coupling agent affects the surface functional groups of CDs and their luminescence properties, which would have a great effect on the sensitive detection of quinoline [32,37]. Therefore, the silane coupling agent needs to be optimized according to the quenching efficiency of different silanized CDS towards quinoline.

The silane coupling agent was optimized by comparing the fluorescence quenching efficiency towards quinoline on different CDs hydrothermally prepared from various silane coupling agents with different N and O contents (KH602, KH550, and KH792). Owing to the higher quenching efficiency (F 0/F) of KH792-silanized CDs, KH792 is selected as the silane coupling agent for subsequent experiments. The content of pyrrole N, namely, the content of amide groups, in KH792-silanized CDs is higher than that in the CDs from KH602 and KH550 [32]. Amide group can affect the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and can also form hydrogen bonds (CON–H…N) with quinoline and, thus, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched. According to the research of Zheng et al. [31] and Xie et al. [32], with the same reagents, the pyrrole N and amide groups in CDs obtained by one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method are similar with those obtained by hydrothermal method. Owing to the more rapid and efficient process, the one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method is used for subsequent experiments. Furthermore, the amide group on the surface of CDs is the electron-donating group, while the N atom of quinoline is the electron-withdrawing group. Consequently, the amide group on the surface of CDs affects their fluorescence properties. When CDs contact with quinoline, charge transfer occurs between the two N atoms. The electron on the N atom on the surface of CDs transfers to quinoline, leading to the change of the surface states on CDs. Then the fluorescence quenching occurs. In brief, optimized CDs were prepared by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, using citric acid as the carbon source, and KH792 as the silane coupling agent, for subsequent synthesis of CD-MIP.

In the preparation of CD-MIP, CDs were used as the nanopomegranate seeds to emit fluorescent signal. The kind of CDs will affect the fluorescence quenching process, while the dosage of CDs will directly affect the sensitivity of CD-MIP towards analyte. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of different dosage of CDs (50, 100, 150 μL; 100 mg mL−1) was given in Fig. S2 (a). When the dosage of CDs is 100 μL, the quenching efficiency is the largest and the detection effect for quinoline is the optimal. Too much a dosage of CDs will obscure the signal because of the strong background noise, but too little a dosage will cause a poor detection effect as a result of insufficient signal output. Therefore, 100 μL dosage of CDs was the optimal dosage to prepare CD-MIP.

On the other hand, the kind and dosage of functional monomer have a great influence on the recognition performance and imprinting effect of CD-MIP. The optimization and simulation of the template-functional monomer system are shown in Fig. S2(b). The template molecule quinoline and functional monomer KH792 form a stable quinoline-KH792 complex, which can deliver shape-memory recognition sites. The energy difference ΔE before and after the formation of quinoline template-KH792 functional monomer assembly is listed in Table 2 . The binding energy change of quinoline template-KH792 functional monomer complex is a negative value (-27.62 kJ mol−1), indicating favourable intermolecular force (hydrogen bonds (NH…N, phenyl–H…N)), low energy, and high stability. Thus, the formation of quinoline template-KH792 functional monomer complex is expected to achieve the specific recognition and selective capture of quinoline.

The dosage of KH792 functional monomer is optimized in Fig. S2(c). When the molar ratio of KH792 to quinoline is 1.5:1, the detection effect is optimal. Lower dosage of KH792 could not fully react with the active sites on the surface of CDs, leading to insufficient imprinted sites and lower detection ability. Excessive KH792 will self-polymerize and hinder the polymerization between KH792 and quinoline, resulting in the reduction of imprinted cavities for specific recognition of quinoline and the decrease of detection ability.

Reaction time has significant effect on the embedment depth of CDs and the number of imprinted cavities, which directly influence the detection performance of CD-MIP. Inadequate reaction time causes insufficient polymerization, which leads to insufficient recognition sites and poor quenching efficiency. Excessive reaction time causes excessive crosslinking, deep CD embedding and deep imprinted sites, which makes it difficult to capture target molecule to interact with CDs effectively and timely, so the quenching efficiency is also reduced. As can be seen from Fig. S2(d), the quenching efficiency CD-MIP with reaction time of 24 h is the best. With this reaction time, CDs are encapsulated in imprinted materials more uniformly, making it easy for quinoline to get access to and interact with CDs. Thus, more quinoline can be captured and detected by CD-MIP.

The TEM images of CDs, CD-MIP, MIP without CDs, and CD-NIP are shown in Fig. 2. As can be seen from Fig. 2(a), CDs are spheroidal with good dispersion. The average particle size is 5–7 nm [31]. From Fig. 2(b) and (d), the imprinted polymer layer encapsulates multiple CDs, resulting in the increase in the particle size of CD-MIP and CD-NIP particle size. For CD-MIP and CD-NIP, the dispersion is decreased, owing to the crosslinking reaction. According to the particle size statistics of CD-MIP and CD-NIP (Fig. S3), the average particle sizes of them are 18.1 and 16.5 nm, respectively. The slightly larger particle size of CD-MIP is due to the imprinted cavity and surface expansion of CD-MIP upon the elution of template molecules [38]. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the diameter of MIP without CDs is obviously larger than that of CD-MIP, and the morphology is more regular. This is because the crosslinker containing silicon self-polymerizes to form silica spheres without CDs as a carrier. This further proves that the imprinted polymer is grafted on the surface of CDs.

The surface functional groups of CDs, CD-MIP, and CD-NIP were analyzed by FTIR to demonstrate the polymerization of CD-MIP, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The absorption band at 3430 cm−1 of CDs is attributed to the –OH stretching vibration. The band disappears in the spectra of CD-MIP and CD-NIP, indicating the formation of polymerized layer by hydrolytic condensation reaction on the surface of CDs. For CD-MIP, the added template was removed by eltuion treatment. Thus, the composition structure of CD-MIP and CD-NIP is the same mostly. Consequently, no obvious difference is observed between CD-MIP and CD-NIP in FTIR spectra. The absorption bands at 1645 and 1564 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of NH, suggesting the presence of amino group. The absorption bands at 1060 and 783 cm−1 reflect the asymmetric stretching of Si–O–Si and the vibrations of Si–O, indicating the polymerization of crosslinker TEOS. The abundant amino and amide groups on the surface of CDs and CD-MIP can form hydrogen bond with quinoline, which demonstrates CD-MIP has the ability of specific recognization and detection towards quinoline. In addition, the functional groups NH, Si–O, and Si–O–Si before and after imprinting keep unchanged, indicating that the structure of CDs is not damaged. So, the fluorescence property is expected to be maintained during the imprinting process.

To further analyze the thermal stability of CD-MIP, the thermogravimetric curves of CDs, CD-MIP, CD-NIP, and MIP without CDs were recorded in N2 atmosphere. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the final weight loss at 800 ˚C of CDs is 49.81 %. The obvious weight loss at approximately 200 ˚C corresponds to the decomposition of –Si–O– bond [39]. The value for MIP without CDs is 53.96 %, owing to the decomposition of polymer. The value for CD-MIP and CD-NIP is 50.85 % and 50.13 %, respectively. These two values are higher than that of CDs and lower than that of MIP without CDs, indicating the grafting of organic polymer on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, the more obvious weight loss of CD-MIP and CD-NIP than that of CDs during 200-400 ˚C is caused by the thermal decomposition of the imprinted (non-imprinted) polymer grafted onto CDs.

The crystal structures of CDs, CD-MIP, and CD-NIP were analyzed by XRD, and the results are shown in Fig. 3(c). CDs, CD-MIP, and CD-NIP have similar diffraction peak at 15°-25°. The broadened peak for (002) crystal plane of graphite indicates their amorphous structures [40]. Notably, the diffraction peak of CD-MIP and CD-NIP is around 22.72°, higher than that for CDs at 21.06°. The increase in diffraction angle is probably caused by the decrease of crystal plane spacing, owing to the coating of CDs by the dense silicon-containing imprinted (non-imprinted) polymer.

The surface element compositions and relative contents of CDs and CD-MIP were further analyzed by elemental analysis on an Elementar instrument. As shown in Table S2, after imprinting, the relative contents of C and N decrease by nearly half and that of Si is nearly doubled. This is because of the presence of crosslinker TEOS. Owing to the grafting and crosslinking on the surface of CDs, Si–O–Si bond increases and the ratio of NH to Si–O–Si decreases.

To investigate the photoluminescence properties of CD-MIP, the emission and excitation spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 4 (a). It can be seen that, when excited at 365 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks are at 450 nm for both materials. The emission and excitation spectra of CD-MIP exhibit similar trends with bare CDs, implying the imprinted polymers on the surface of CDs have little effect on the optical properties, because the imprinted polymer layer is optically transparent and inert [41]. As shown in Fig. 4(b), CD-NIP exhibits strong fluorescence intensity. For CD-MIP, before quinoline extraction, the fluorescence intensity of CD-MIP is effectively quenched. However, after elution, most of quinoline molecules are removed from the imprinted cavities, and the fluorescence intensity resumes nearly to that of CD-NIP. The fluorescence intensity of CD-MIP is quenched again when quioline is further added, which demonstrates that CD-MIP can remove and recombine the target molecule quinoline effectively, and thus is feasible to detect quinoline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of CD-MIP for quinoline
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism based on the fluorescence quenching in CD-MIP system can be drawn from the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. As depicted in Fig. 4(c), no overlap is observed between the UV–vis spectra of quinoline molecule and the fluorescence emission spectra of CD-MIP, excluding the energy transfer between them [37,42]. Similarly, no overlap is observed between the UV–vis spectra of quinoline molecule and the fluorescence excitation spectra of CD-MIP, excluding the inner filter effect [43,44]. For UV–vis spectra of quinoline molecule and CDs, the upward and downward trends and the positions of the peaks are almost the same, suggesting the charge transfer from CDs to quinoline molecule is a possible mechanism [37,45].

The plausible fluorescence quenching mechanism is shown in Fig. 5 . Under UV irradiation, the electrons from CDs absorb energy and transit from the ground state to the excited state. If the CDs are treated as a semiconductor, the excited electrons would return to the valence band (VB) from conduction band (CB), accompanying the occurrence of fluorescence. As depicted in Fig. 5(a), when quinoline is recognized and captured into the CD-MIP system through imprinted cavities with specific recognition sites, it contacts CDs, then, they are bonded together through hydrogen bond. On account of the electron-withdrawing nature of the N atom from quinoline molecule [13,46], the excited electrons in CB are transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of quinoline, resulting in fluorescence quenching of CD-MIP (Fig. 5(b)). In this process, quinoline molecule and CD-MIP act as electron receptor and donor, respectively. In brief, the sensing mechanism based on the fluorescence quenching in CD-MIP system may be the charge transfer.

The fluorescence stability is a critical index to evaluate whether CD-MIP can be utilized for practical detection. As shown in Fig. S4(a), under continuous UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity almost keeps stable within 180 min, which indicates that CD-MIP has excellent UV resistance. In addition, the CD-MIP was stored at 4 ˚C for 10 days, and its fluorescence intensity was recorded for each day, the results are shown in Fig. S4(b). The fluorescence intensity remains above 90 % after 10 days, implying the superior optical stability of CD-MIP.

An appropriate external condition is vital for sensitive and selective detection, including the dosage of detection material, pH, temperature, response time, and so on. In order to evaluate the effect of CD-MIP concentration on the detection performance for quinoline, the quenching efficiency of different concentrations (0.5–2 mg mL−1) of CD-MIP upon the addition of quinoline (100 mg/L) was determined. As given in Fig. S5(a), 1 mg mL-1CD-MIP has the highest quenching efficiency. Lower concentration (0.5 mL−1) of CD-MIP would reduce the sensitivity owing to the reduction of signal-to-noise ratio. Higher concentration (1.5 and 2.0 mg mL−1) would cause the fluorescence self-absorption and the decrease in sensitivity accordingly.

The pH value of aqueous solution has a noteworthy influence on the quenching efficiency of CD-MIP for quinoline. The quenching efficiency of CD-MIP for quinoline at different pH values is shown in Fig. S5(b). Obviously, CD-MIP has the greatest quenching degree when pH value is 7. When pH reaches 8, the quenching efficiency decreases slightly. When pH decreases or increases to other values, the quenching efficiency decreases significantly. This phenomenon can be explained as the protonation and deprotonation process [37]. Under acidic conditions, the solution is rich in H+, so there is more positive charge on the surface of CD-MIP. Quinoline is of weakly alkaline nature and mainly exists in the form of cations in aqueous solution. The electrostatic repulsion between CD-MIP and quinoline results in the decrease of CD-MIP affinity for quinoline and consequently the decline in detection performance [4,13,47]. Under alkaline conditions, the functional groups (–COOH, –CONH), which can interact with quinoline, on CDs and CD-MIP surface may be destroyed, which hinders the interaction with quinoline, resulting in the degradation of detection performance. In general, under the neutral or weakly alkaline condition, the detection performance maintains relatively stable. Because the pH value of actual coking wastewater is weakly alkaline (pH≈8), CD-MIP is expected to be applicable in the real environment.

The response time of CD-MIP towards quinoline is displayed in Fig. S5(c). It is obvious that the quenching efficiency is basically stabilized within 90 s, which is ascribed to the high mass transfer efficiency due to the good accessibility of the imprinted cavities on the surface of CD-MIP to quinoline. Therefore, CD-MIP can quickly generate fluorescence response towards quinoline molecule.

In order to evaluate the affinity of CD-MIP and CD-NIP to the target molecule, the fluorescence response of CD-MIP and CD-NIP towards quinoline at various concentrations was investigated. Shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b) are the fluorescence spectra of CD-MIP and CD-NIP with the increase of quinoline concentration, respectively. Although the fluorescence intensity of both CD-MIP and CD-NIP is gradually quenched with the increase of quinoline concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CD-MIP is substantially better than that of CD-NIP. This is because the imprinted cavities on CD-MIP surface highly match quinoline molecule in spatial arrangement and chemical structure, therefore realizing specific recognition. However, there is no imprinted cavity on CD-NIP surface, and thus only a small amount of quinoline molecule is adsorbed. The results further demonstrate the excellent affinity of CD-MIP towards quinoline.

In addition, as evident in Fig. 6(c), the fluorescence intensity quenching of CD-MIP and CD-NIP shows a linear relationship with increasing concentration of quinoline. The relationship conforms to the Stern–Volmer equation as follows [48,49]:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
C
quinoline

where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CD-MIP before and after binding to quinoline molecule, respectively; K SV is the Stern–Volmer constant (L mg−1); C quinoline is the concentration of quinoline (mg/L). The corresponding linear fitting equation of CD-MIP is F 0/F = 1 + 7.55 × 10-4 C quinoline, R2 = 0.9999. The corresponding linear fitting equation of CD-NIP is F 0/F = 1 + 2.18 × 10-4 C quinoline, R2 = 0.9999. The result indicates that the quenching process belongs to a single form of fluorescence quenching process [50]. The linear range of the sensor covers 20–200 mg/L. In addition, according to the 3σ/K criterion (σ is the blank measurement standard deviation (n = 5) and K is the slope of the calibration curve) [44,51]. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 6.7 mg/L. The imprinted factor (IF), one of the crucial indicators to assess the selectivity of CD-MIP, is obtained by the definition IF = K SV,CD-MIP/K SV,CD-NIP. K SV,CD-MIP and K SV,CD-NIP were calculated as 0.755 and 0.218 L g−1, respectively [52]. Thus IF is obtained as 3.46. The results show that the sensitivity of CD-MIP to quinoline molecule is much higher than that of CD-NIP. This is mainly attributed to the specific and selective binding of quinoline in CD-MIP. Thus, CD-MIP has favorable specific recognition ability and selectivity towards quinoline.

Regenerability is a significant factor in assessing the application potential of CD-MIP. To investigate the recyclability of CD-MIP, six removal–rebinding cycles were performed, and the fluorescence intensity was measured after each cycle. In brief, the obtained CD-MIP was dissolved in deionized water to prepare a work solution with a concentration of 1 mg mL−1. The fluorescence intensity was measured before and after quinoline addition (200 mg/L). The fluorescence quenching rate (F0/F) was calculated. After each removal–rebinding cycle, CD-MIP was separated by rotary evaporation, washed with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, by volume), and collected by centrifugation. The regenerated CD-MIP was reused in the next cycle of detection experiments. The recyclability is shown in Fig. S6. Evidently, after six removal–rebinding cycles, the fluorescence intensity of CD-MIP is remained above 95 %. More importantly, the value of fluorescence quenching rate (F0/F) is basically unchanged. This result indicates that the regenerated CD-MIP still has excellent detection ability towards quinoline, and the excellent regenerability is proved.

The selective specificity of CD-MIP is the ability to resist interference in a complex environment. Phenol, pyridine, naphthalene, and carbazole (the concentration of each substance was 100 mg/L) were selected as interferents owing to their high content and similar structure with quinoline molecule in coking wastewater [53,54]. The selective detection capacity of CD-MIP and CD-NIP were determined. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the quenching efficiency for quinoline is much higher than that for interferents. This is due to the large number of imprinted cavities in CD-MIP which have strong chemical and structural affinity for quinoline. These cavities only allow quinoline to enter the polymer system and contact with CDs, while isolating other substances and impeding their contact with CDs. Therefore, CD-MIP can effectively detect quinoline rather than other molecules. On the other hand, no imprinted cavities exist in CD-NIP for efficient recognization and capturing of quinoline. Therefore, the change in fluorescence signal only relies on the loose polymeric network that allows quinoline or interferent molecules to enter and contact with CDs, so the detection of CD-NIP has no selectivity towards quinoline.

To verify the anti-interference ability of CD-MIP in complex environment, four mixed solutions of quinoline + phenol, quinoline + pyridine, quinoline + naphthalene, and quinoline + carbazole (C quinoline = C phenol = C pyridine = C naphthalene = C carbazole = 100 mg/L) were prepared to simulate coking wastewater, the quenching efficiency were determined. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the addition of the interferents has negligible effect on the fluorescence response of CD-MIP to quinoline, which can be ascribed to the specificity of imprinted cavities towards quinoline. Therefore, CD-MIP has a good affinity for quinoline molecule, and exhibits an outstanding fluorescence response and anti-interference ability. In summary, CD-MIP possesses the applicability and validity to be employed to detect quinoline in coking wastewater.

The feasibility and accuracy of CD-MIP detection method in real coking wastewater was investigated. As displayed in Table 2, the recovery is between 98.45 % and 104.14 %, and the RSD ranges from 2.8 % to 3.9 %, proving the satisfactory reproducibility. Thus, the developed CD-MIP has good accuracy and reliability for the quantitative detection of quinoline in real coking wastewater.

As shown in Table 3 , various detection methods for NHCs in water are summarized and listed. Compared with other detection methods, CD-MIP fluorescence detection method exhibits a suitable detection range. According to statistics, after the advanced treatment, the concentration of NHCs in coking wastewater is lower than 200 mg/L, and the emission standard of NHCs pollutants for coking chemical industry is 10 mg/L. To achieve this detection requirement, other detection methods require a variety of pre-treatment. Owing to the high concentration in detection range (HPLC) [55], the wastewater needs further enrichment treatment. Owing to the low concentration in detection range (electrochemical [56,57]; MS [58]), the wastewater needs further dilution treatment. The detection range obtained by our work is exactly within the concentration range required for detection. Therefore, the wastewater can be detected directly without further treatment. Generally, compared with other fluorescence materials [59], CDs show excellent fluorescence characteristics. CDs-based fluorescence method exhibits good sensitivity (low LOD), wide detection range, and fast response. However, owing to the lack of matched molecule cavities, CDs-based fluorescence method is not selective to the target analytes [60,61]. In our work, the introduction of molecular imprinting endows the CD-MIPs with excellent selectivity and high detection efficiency, thus making it highly applicable to the detection of NHCs. In addition, CD-MIP obtained in this work exhibits fast response and good anti-interference ability. Thus, CD-MIP is expected to act as a sensor for the fast and direct detection of quinoline in coking wastewater.

A novel molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanopomegranate CD-MIP was developed with carbon dots as the seeds for fluorescence signal, quinoline as the template, and KH792 as the functional monomer. CD-MIP has a regular spherical shape with a diameter of around 18.1 nm, and possesses considerably stable fluorescence performance. The detection process is mainly based on the charge transfer between the –CONH group of CDs and the N atom of quinoline molecule. Under the optimal conditions, the detection capacity of CD-MIP for quinoline is significantly higher than that of its non-imprinted counterpart CD-NIP. In addition, CD-MIP exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity for quinoline with a fast fluorescence response within 90 s. The imprinted factor for quinoline against CD-NIP is 3.46. CD-MIP possesses considerable detection performance for quinoline under neutral and weakly alkaline conditions, which is suitable for quinoline detection in real coking wastewater. Therefore, CD-MIP could be an extremely promising detector in coking wastewater.
